target.name: Dactyloquinone A
target.motivation: Dactyloquinone A is a member of the avarane subfamily of sesquiterpene (hydro)quinone meroterpenoids. Prior hemisynthesis produced a mixture of isomers, and a total synthesis was desired to confirm its structure and provide access to related compounds.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: silver-mediated cationic cyclization of a 5,10-unsaturated aureane skeleton onto the quinone moiety, accompanied by a 1,2-methyl shift, which produced a mixture of isomers

success_criteria.free_text: Confirmation of the structure by single X-ray diffraction.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-O bond formation between the C10-carbon and the quinone moiety
key_disconnections.reasoning: This strategy was chosen to regioselectively functionalize the C10-carbon with the quinone moiety, leading to the dactyloquinone A skeleton. This approach was inspired by previous work on C-O bond formation of exo-glycals with 1,4-quinones under iron-hydride hydrogen atom transfer conditions.
Oxidation of a para-methoxy phenol with diacetoxyiodobenzene (PIDA) in methanol was chosen to generate the quinone monoacetal, which was found to be a suitable substrate for the MHAT process.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Deconjugative alkylation of a Wieland–Miescher type ketone
key_disconnections.reasoning: This disconnection allows for the construction of the meroterpenoid scaffold, providing the necessary complexity and stereochemistry at an early stage.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The overall strategy involves building the meroterpenoid scaffold from a Wieland–Miescher type ketone analog, followed by deconjugative alkylation with a functionalized aromatic part. A key step is the intramolecular MHAT process to form the C-O bond of the chromane moiety. The synthesis of dactyloquinone A was pursued first, aiming to connect the C10-carbon with the quinone derivative. The strategy for spiroetherone A involved a quinol–enedione rearrangement, which was developed in parallel.

thought.background: The Wieland–Miescher ketone analog 15 is a known enantioenriched starting material.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize the enantioenriched Wieland–Miescher ketone analog 15 from simpler precursors.
thought.decision: To obtain 15, a Hajos–Parrish–Eder–Sauer–Wiechert reaction with (L)-phenylalanine and pyridinium p-toluenesulfonate (PPTS) was employed, followed by acetal formation.

outcome.observation: Enantioenriched Wieland–Miescher ketone analog 15 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Following Z. Lu’s approach,[8,9] compounds related to 8 were obtained from enantioenriched Wieland–Miescher ketone analog 15 obtained in three steps from 2-methylcyclohexa-1,3-dione 13 and pent-1-ene-3-one 14 via a Hajos–Parrish–Eder–Sauer–Wiechert reaction with (L)-phenylalanine ((L)-phe) and pyridinium p-toluenesulfonate (PPTS) (Scheme 2).

thought.background: Enone 15 is available. Benzyl bromides 16a,b are masked quinones.
thought.gap: Need to introduce the aromatic moiety via deconjugative alkylation.
thought.decision: To introduce the aromatic part, deconjugative alkylation of enone 15 with benzyl bromides 16a,b was performed.

outcome.observation: Ketones 17a,b were obtained.

evidence.text_span: Deconjugative alkylation of enone 15 with benzyl bromides 16a,b, as masked quinones led to ketones 17a,b.

thought.background: Ketones 17a,b are available.
thought.gap: Need to convert the ketone to an exocyclic alkene and deprotect the acetal.
thought.decision: Wittig olefination and acetal hydrolysis were performed to yield 18 and 20.

outcome.observation: Compounds 18 and 20 were obtained.

evidence.text_span: Wittig olefination and acetal hydrolysis into 18 and 20 were followed by deprotection of the phenols.

thought.background: Compounds 18 and 20 are available.
thought.gap: Need to deprotect the phenols.
thought.decision: Deprotection of the phenols was performed using NaSEt.

outcome.observation: Deprotected phenols 19 and 21 were obtained.

evidence.text_span: Wittig olefination and acetal hydrolysis into 18 and 20 were followed by deprotection of the phenols.

thought.background: Phenols 19 and 21 are available.
thought.gap: Need to regioselectively hydrogenate the substituted exocyclic alkene without affecting the trisubstituted endocyclic one.
thought.decision: The Wilkinson catalyst was used for regioselective hydrogenation.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation proceeded with 1.6:1 diastereoselectivity, and diastereoisomers 22a/22b and 23a/23b were separated by chromatography.

evidence.text_span: The use of the Wilkinson catalyst allowed the regioselective hydrogenation of the substituted exocyclic alkene of 19 and 21 without affecting the trisubstituted endocyclic one. It proceeded with a 1.6:1 diastereoselectivity and the diastereoisomers 22a/22b and 23a/23b were easily separated by chromatography on silica gel due to the presence of the free phenol.

thought.background: Diastereoisomers 22a and 22b are available.
thought.gap: Need to oxidize the phenol to the corresponding quinones.
thought.decision: Oxidation of the phenol of 22a and 22b with salcomine was performed.

outcome.observation: Quinones 24a and 24b were delivered.

evidence.text_span: Then, oxidation of the phenol of 22a and 22b with salcomine delivered the corresponding quinones 24a and 24b

thought.background: Diastereoisomers 23a and 23b are available.
thought.gap: Need to oxidize the para-methoxy phenols to quinone monoacetals.
thought.decision: Oxidation of the para-methoxy phenols of 23a and 23b with diacetoxyiodobenzene (PIDA) in methanol was performed.

outcome.observation: Quinone monoacetals (QMA) 25a and 25b were generated.

evidence.text_span: while oxidation of the para-methoxy phenols of 23a and 23b with diacetoxyiodobenzene (PIDA) in methanol generated the quinone monoacetals (QMA) 25a and 25b.

thought.background: Quinone 24b is available. The goal is to perform an intramolecular MHAT process on the C1–C10 alkene and regioselectively functionalize the C10-carbon with the quinone moiety.
thought.gap: Need to find conditions for the MHAT process that lead to the desired C-O bond formation.
thought.decision: Attempted MHAT process on quinone 24b with iron acetylacetonate and phenylsilane.

outcome.observation: Quinone 24b was reduced to its hydroquinone, and the C1=C10 double bond was fully reduced to 26. The desired C-O bond formation did not occur.

evidence.text_span: As a representative example, with iron acetylacetonate and phenylsilane, quinone 24b was unfortunately reduced into its hydroquinone while the C1=C10 double bond was fully reduced into 26.

thought.background: The MHAT process on quinone 24b led to undesired reduction. Quinone monoacetals (QMAs) are also available.
thought.gap: Need to find a substrate that undergoes the desired C-O bond formation under MHAT conditions.
thought.decision: Tested QMA 25a under the same MHAT conditions, hypothesizing that the QMA motif might favor C-O bond formation over reduction.

outcome.observation: QMA 25a underwent functionalization at the C10-carbon through the reaction of the carbonyl group of the QMA motif, leading to the chemoselective formation of a C─O bond and providing access to the dactyloquinone A skeleton 27.

evidence.text_span: Surprisingly, under the same reaction conditions, QMA 25a underwent functionalization at the C10-carbon through the reaction of the carbonyl group of the QMA motif, leading to the chemoselective formation of a C─O bond and providing access to the dactyloquinone A skeleton 27.

thought.background: The MHAT process on QMA 25a successfully formed the dactyloquinone A skeleton 27. However, the dialkylated hydroquinone part of 27 is not sufficiently functionalized for the total synthesis of dactyloquinone A.
thought.gap: Need to introduce a more functionalized aromatic part at an earlier stage.
thought.decision: Decided to synthesize a more functionalized benzyl iodide 31 from 2,4,5-trimethoxybenzaldehyde 28.

outcome.observation: Formylated product 29 was obtained after Baeyer–Villiger oxidation and subsequent formylation.

evidence.text_span: Baeyer–Villiger oxidation of 2,4,5-trimethoxybenzaldehyde 28, followed by subsequent formylation led to 29.

thought.background: Compound 29 is available.
thought.gap: Need to protect the phenol and reduce the aldehyde to a benzylic alcohol.
thought.decision: Phenol protection with TBSCl and aldehyde reduction with NaBH4 were performed.

outcome.observation: Benzylic alcohol 30 was furnished.

evidence.text_span: Phenol protection and aldehyde reduction furnished benzylic alcohol 30

thought.background: Benzylic alcohol 30 is available.
thought.gap: Need to convert the benzylic alcohol to the corresponding benzyl iodide.
thought.decision: Conversion to benzyl bromide with CBr4/PPh3 followed by Finkelstein reaction with NaI was performed.

outcome.observation: Benzyl iodide 31 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: which was converted into the corresponding benzyl bromide and then benzyl iodide 31 via a Finkelstein reaction.

thought.background: Benzyl iodide 31 and enone 15 are available.
thought.gap: Need to perform the deconjugative alkylation with the new functionalized aromatic part.
thought.decision: Deconjugative alkylation of 15 with 31 was performed.

outcome.observation: The expected diastereoisomer 32 was isolated pure after chromatography on silica gel, with a 7.5:1 diastereoselectivity at C9. Regioisomeric alkylation at C7 was observed as a minor isomer.

evidence.text_span: The deconjugative alkylation of 15 with the latter proceeded with a 7.5:1 diastereoselectivity at C9 and the regioisomeric alkylation at C7 was also observed as a minor isomer. The expected diastereoisomer 32 was isolated pure after chromatography on silica gel.

thought.background: Compound 32 is available.
thought.gap: Need to perform olefination of the ketone and successive deprotection of the ketone and the phenol.
thought.decision: Olefination was performed under Takai–Oshima–Lombardo conditions, followed by deprotection steps.

outcome.observation: Compound 33 was yielded.

evidence.text_span: Olefination of the ketone was performed under the Takai–Oshima–Lombardo[28,29] conditions and successive deprotection of the ketone and the phenol yielded 33.

thought.background: Compound 33 is available.
thought.gap: Need to hydrogenate the exocyclic alkene regioselectively.
thought.decision: Hydrogenation with the Wilkinson catalyst was performed.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation proceeded with a 1.5:1 diastereoisomeric ratio favoring 34a. The diastereoisomers were separated.

evidence.text_span: Hydrogenation with the Wilkinson catalyst proceeded with a 1.5:1 diastereoisomeric ratio favoring 34a. At this stage, the diastereoisomers were separated.

thought.background: The desired diastereoisomer 34a is available. The MHAT conditions for C-O bond formation with QMA were previously established.
thought.gap: Need to oxidize 34a to the QMA and then perform the MHAT process to form the chromane moiety.
thought.decision: Oxidation of 34a with PIDA in methanol was performed, followed by the MHAT conditions.

outcome.observation: A mixture of ortho- and para-QMA was delivered, which under the MHAT conditions enabled the formation of the desired C─O bond and of the chromane moiety of 35.

evidence.text_span: Oxidation of the desired one 34a with PIDA in methanol delivered a mixture of the corresponding ortho- and para-QMA which, under the MHAT conditions we developed, enabled the formation of the desired C─O bond and of the chromane moiety of 35.

thought.background: Compound 35, containing the chromane moiety, is available.
thought.gap: Need to complete the total synthesis of dactyloquinone A by forming the exocyclic alkene and oxidizing the benzene ring to a quinone.
thought.decision: Wittig olefination of the ketone and oxidation of the highly electron-rich benzene ring to a quinone with ceric ammonium nitrate (CAN) were performed.

outcome.observation: Dactyloquinone A (2) was yielded, and its structure was confirmed by single X-ray diffraction.

evidence.text_span: Finally, Wittig olefination of the ketone and oxidation of the highly electron-rich benzene ring to a quinone with ceric ammonium nitrate (CAN) yielded dactyloquinone A (2), the structure of which was confirmed by single X-ray diffraction.

thought.background: The synthesis of dactyloquinone A was successful. The next goal is to investigate the connection of the C1-carbon with the quinone to access spirocycles.
thought.gap: Need to find conditions for acid-mediated cyclization of quinones or QMAs to form quinols or spirocycles.
thought.decision: Attempted acid-mediated cyclization of quinones 24a,b or QMA 25a,b.

outcome.observation: Attempted acid-mediated cyclization did not furnish quinols 36 or spirocycles 37 but only led to complex product mixtures.

evidence.text_span: Unfortunately, despite extensive study, attempted acid-mediated cyclization of quinones 24a,b or QMA 25a,b did not furnish quinols 36 or spirocycles 37 but only led to complex product mixtures (Scheme 4).

thought.background: Direct acid-mediated cyclization to form spirocycles was unsuccessful.
thought.gap: Need an alternative strategy to access the spirocyclic motif of septosones B, C or spiroetherones A, B.
thought.decision: Decided to revise the synthetic plan and access the spirocycle via a stereospecific quinol–enedione rearrangement of 9 to 10. This requires obtaining quinol 9 first, which could be achieved via aryl radical cyclization from para-bromophenol 38, followed by oxidation.

outcome.observation: Revised synthetic plan to use a quinol–enedione rearrangement.

evidence.text_span: Therefore, we decided to revise our synthetic plan and to access the spirocycle of septosones B, C (6a,b) or spiroetherones A, B (7a,b) via a stereospecific quinol–enedione rearrangement of 9 to 10 (Scheme 5).

thought.background: The revised plan requires para-bromophenol 38. A suitable precursor is 40.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize the benzyl bromide 41 from 40.
thought.decision: Benzylic bromination of 40 with N-bromosuccinimide (NBS) was performed.

outcome.observation: Benzyl bromide 41 was delivered.

evidence.text_span: To this end, benzylic bromination of 40 with N-bromosuccinimide (NBS) delivered benzyl bromide 41[41] which was then used in the deconjugative alkylation step of enone 15.

thought.background: Benzyl bromide 41 and enone 15 are available.
thought.gap: Need to perform the deconjugative alkylation with 41.
thought.decision: Deconjugative alkylation of enone 15 with 41 was performed.

outcome.observation: Compound 42 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: which was then used in the deconjugative alkylation step of enone 15. Subjection of 42 to tributyltin hydride and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) delivered regioselectively and diastereoselectively tetracycle 43.

thought.background: Compound 42 is available.
thought.gap: Need to perform the aryl radical cyclization to form the tetracycle.
thought.decision: Subjection of 42 to tributyltin hydride and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) was performed.

outcome.observation: Tetracycle 43 was delivered regioselectively and diastereoselectively.

evidence.text_span: Subjection of 42 to tributyltin hydride and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) delivered regioselectively and diastereoselectively tetracycle 43.

thought.background: Tetracycle 43 is available.
thought.gap: Need to transform the ketone into a methyl group via Wittig olefination and diastereoselective hydrogenation, with acetal hydrolysis in between.
thought.decision: Wittig olefination of 43 was performed, followed by acetal hydrolysis and diastereoselective hydrogenation.

outcome.observation: Compound 44 was obtained after Wittig olefination.

evidence.text_span: The ketone was then transformed into a methyl group via Wittig olefination of 43 and diastereoselective hydrogenation of 44 with acetal hydrolysis between these two events.

thought.background: Compound 44 is available.
thought.gap: Need to perform acetal hydrolysis and diastereoselective hydrogenation.
thought.decision: Acetal hydrolysis with HCl and diastereoselective hydrogenation with Pd/C and H2 were performed.

outcome.observation: Compound 45 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: The ketone was then transformed into a methyl group via Wittig olefination of 43 and diastereoselective hydrogenation of 44 with acetal hydrolysis between these two events.

thought.background: Anisole 45 is available.
thought.gap: Need to demethylate the anisole to deliver the phenol.
thought.decision: Demethylation of the anisole 45 was performed using lithium diphenylphosphide.

outcome.observation: Phenol 46 was delivered.

evidence.text_span: Demethylation of the anisole 45 delivered phenol 46.

thought.background: Phenol 46 is available.
thought.gap: Need to convert phenol 46 to quinol 47 with the necessary stereochemistry for the quinol–enedione rearrangement.
thought.decision: Photooxygenation with 1,8-dihydroxyanthraquinone as photosensitizer was performed.

outcome.observation: Diastereoselective conversion to quinol 47 was achieved, possessing the stereochemistry necessary to access the dysideanones A, E and spiroetherones A, B.

evidence.text_span: Photooxygenation[42–44] with 1,8-dihydroxyanthraquinone as photosensitizer allowed for diastereoselective conversion to quinol 47, which possesses the stereochemistry necessary to access the dysideanones A, E (5a,b) and spiroetherones A, B (7a,b), according to the stereospecificity of the rearrangement.

thought.background: Quinol 47 is available. The goal is to perform the quinol–spiroenedione rearrangement.
thought.gap: Need to find suitable conditions for the quinol–spiroenedione rearrangement.
thought.decision: Treated 47 with boron trifluoride etherate, based on a precedent by Davis.

outcome.observation: Did not observe the targeted spiroenedione 48. Instead, hydroquinone 49 was isolated, resulting from a formal 1,3-shift of the C1-carbon to the C21-carbon of the quinol.

evidence.text_span: Unfortunately, when we treated 47 with boron trifluoride etherate, we did not observe the targeted spiroenedione 48. Instead, we isolated hydroquinone 49, which resulted from a formal 1,3-shift of the C1-carbon to the C21-carbon of the quinol.

thought.background: The quinol–enedione rearrangement of 47 with BF3·OEt2 led to an undesired 1,3-shift. Also, selective olefination of the C4-ketone over the enedione motif might be problematic if the spiroenedione 48 were isolated.
thought.gap: Need to perform the olefination step before the formation of the quinol and find milder conditions for the rearrangement.
thought.decision: Decided to perform the olefination step before quinol formation. Wittig reaction with ketone 45 was planned to deliver exo-methylene compound 50. Also, considered protecting the alkene as a primary alcohol to avoid interference with rearrangement conditions.

outcome.observation: Revised strategy to perform olefination earlier and consider alkene protection.

evidence.text_span: Nevertheless, at this point, we realized that, even if spiroenedione 48 could be isolated, selective olefination of the C4-ketone over the carbonyls in the enedione motif might be problematic. Consequently, we decided to perform the olefination step before the formation of the quinol. Thus, Wittig reaction with ketone 45 delivered the corresponding exo-methylene compound 50. At this point, we were also concerned that the conditions that we intended to employ for the promotion of the quinol–enedione rearrangement would interfere with this alkene. Therefore, we envisioned protecting the latter as a primary alcohol that could be subjected to elimination at a later stage.

thought.background: Ketone 45 is available. The revised strategy involves performing olefination before quinol formation.
thought.gap: Need to convert the ketone to an exocyclic alkene.
thought.decision: Wittig reaction with ketone 45 was performed.

outcome.observation: Exo-methylene compound 50 was delivered.

evidence.text_span: Thus, Wittig reaction with ketone 45 delivered the corresponding exo-methylene compound 50.

thought.background: Exo-methylene compound 50 is available. Concerns exist about the alkene interfering with rearrangement conditions.
thought.gap: Need to protect the alkene as a primary alcohol.
thought.decision: Hydroboration of 50 with 9-BBN was followed by oxidation and protection of the primary alcohol as silyl ether 51.

outcome.observation: Silyl ether 51 was obtained after hydroboration, oxidation, and protection. Diastereomeric ratio of 5:1 was observed.

evidence.text_span: Thus, hydroboration of 50 with 9- borabicyclo[3.3.1]nonane (9-BBN) was followed by oxidation and protection of the primary alcohol as silyl ether 51.

thought.background: Silyl ether 51 is available.
thought.gap: Need to demethylate the anisole to obtain the phenol.
thought.decision: Phenol 52 was obtained by demethylation of the anisole employing lithium diphenylphosphide.

outcome.observation: Phenol 52 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Phenol 52 was obtained by demethylation of the anisole employing lithium diphenylphosphide.

thought.background: Phenol 52 is available.
thought.gap: Need to convert phenol 52 to the quinol 53.
thought.decision: Photooxygenation with 1,8-dihydroxyanthraquinone as photosensitizer was performed.

outcome.observation: The expected quinol 53 was delivered.

evidence.text_span: Photooxygenation with 1,8- dihydroxyanthraquinone as photosensitizer then delivered the expected quinol 53.

thought.background: Quinol 53 is available. The previous attempt at quinol–enedione rearrangement with BF3·OEt2 was unsuccessful.
thought.gap: Need to find conditions for the quinol–enedione rearrangement that avoid the 1,3-migration and lead to the spirocyclic enedione.
thought.decision: Attempted to promote the rearrangement by forming an iodonium ion with one of the double bonds of the quinol, treating 53 with iodine and PIDA.

outcome.observation: The desired iodide-containing spirocyclic enedione 54 was observed by 1H NMR of the crude reaction mixture, but it was unstable and could not be isolated.

evidence.text_span: Satisfactorily, treatment of 53 with iodine and PIDA led to the observation of the desired iodide-containing spirocyclic enedione 54 by 1HNMR of the crude reaction mixture. Unfortunately, this compound was unstable and could not be isolated.

thought.background: The iodide-containing spirocyclic enedione 54 was unstable. The goal is to reduce the C─I bond immediately after its formation.
thought.gap: Need to find suitable conditions for the reduction of the C─I bond without overreduction.
thought.decision: Attempted to reduce the C─I bond using tributyltin hydride/AIBN.

outcome.observation: Both tributyltin hydride/AIBN led to overreduction and yielded 1,4-dione derivative 56.

evidence.text_span: Thus, we decided to immediately reduce the C─I bond. Both tributyltin hydride/AIBN and samarium diiodide led to overreduction and yielded 1,4-dione derivative 56.

thought.background: The iodide-containing spirocyclic enedione 54 was unstable. Reduction with tributyltin hydride/AIBN led to overreduction.
thought.gap: Need to find suitable conditions for the reduction of the C─I bond without overreduction.
thought.decision: Attempted to reduce the C─I bond using samarium diiodide.

outcome.observation: Samarium diiodide also led to overreduction and yielded 1,4-dione derivative 56.

evidence.text_span: Thus, we decided to immediately reduce the C─I bond. Both tributyltin hydride/AIBN and samarium diiodide led to overreduction and yielded 1,4-dione derivative 56.

thought.background: The iodide-containing spirocyclic enedione 54 was unstable, and previous reduction attempts led to overreduction.
thought.gap: Need to find a method to reduce the C─I bond to obtain the desired spirocyclic enedione 55 without overreduction.
thought.decision: Used tributyltin hydride in combination with triethylborane and oxygen for the reduction.

outcome.observation: The desired spirocyclic enedione compound 55 was isolated in 40% over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Fortunately, using tributyltin hydride in combination with triethylborane and oxygen allowed us to isolate the desired spirocyclic enedione compound 55 in 40% over two steps.

thought.background: The two-step procedure for quinol–enedione rearrangement via iodonium ion formation and reduction was successful but low yielding. The previous attempt with BF3·OEt2 led to 1,3-migration. The goal is to find a direct, milder method for the quinol–enedione rearrangement.
thought.gap: Need to evaluate basic conditions for the direct conversion of 53 to 55.
thought.decision: Evaluated basic conditions such as sodium methoxide for the direct conversion of 53 to 55.

outcome.observation: Unsuccessfully evaluated basic conditions such as sodium methoxide for the direct conversion of 53 to 55.

evidence.text_span: In lieu of this two steps procedure, we have also unsuccessfully evaluated basic conditions such as sodium methoxide for the direct conversion of 53 to 55.

thought.background: Previous attempts at quinol–enedione rearrangement were either unsuccessful or low yielding. The goal is to find a direct, milder method for the quinol–enedione rearrangement.
thought.gap: Need to test milder acidic conditions than boron trifluoride etherate.
thought.decision: Tested para-toluene sulfonic acid as a Brønsted acid.

outcome.observation: para-Toluene sulfonic acid as Brønsted acid led to an unknown undesired compound.

evidence.text_span: Next, we considered testing milder acidic conditions than boron trifluoride etherate. para-Toluene sulfonic acid as Brønsted acid led to an unknown undesired compound.

thought.background: Previous attempts at quinol–enedione rearrangement were unsuccessful or led to undesired products. The goal is to find a direct, milder method for the quinol–enedione rearrangement.
thought.gap: Need to find an effective acidic catalyst for the quinol–enedione rearrangement.
thought.decision: Discovered that (L)-camphorsulfonic acid ((L)-CSA) successfully promoted the expected quinol–enedione rearrangement of 53 into 55.

outcome.observation: (L)-camphorsulfonic acid ((L)-CSA) successfully promoted the expected quinol–enedione rearrangement of 53 into 55 in 79% yield.

evidence.text_span: Eventually, we discovered that (L)- camphorsulfonic acid ((L)-CSA) successfully promoted the expected quinol–enedione rearrangement of 53 into 55 in 79% yield.

thought.background: The quinol–enedione rearrangement to 55 was successful. The next step is to synthesize dysideanone G (4b) and dysideanone E (5b) to test the compatibility of the exocyclic double bond with the rearrangement conditions.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize dysideanone G (4b) from 45.
thought.decision: Dysideanone G (4b) was synthesized from 45 via Wittig olefination and demethylation.

outcome.observation: Dysideanone G (4b) was synthesized very efficiently.

evidence.text_span: Thus, dysideanone G (4b) was synthesized very efficiently from 45 via Wittig olefination and demethylation (Scheme 6).

thought.background: Dysideanone G (4b) is available.
thought.gap: Need to convert dysideanone G (4b) to dysideanone E (5b) via photooxygenation.
thought.decision: Photooxygenation of the phenol with rose bengal as a photosensitizer was performed.

outcome.observation: Dysideanone E (5b) was obtained diastereoselectively.

evidence.text_span: Photooxygenation[42] with rose bengal as a photosensitizer then led diastereoselectively to dysideanone E (5b).

thought.background: Dysideanone E (5b) is available. The quinol–enedione rearrangement with (L)-CSA was successful for 53.
thought.gap: Need to perform the quinol–enedione rearrangement on dysideanone E (5b) to obtain the spirocyclic enedione 57.
thought.decision: Treatment of dysideanone E (5b) with (L)-CSA was performed.

outcome.observation: Spirocyclic enedione 57 was yielded in 87%, consistent with the stereochemistry of the spiroetherones.

evidence.text_span: To our delight, treatment of the latter quinol with (L)-CSA yielded in 87% the spirocyclic enedione 57, consistent with the stereochemistry of the spiroetherones.

thought.background: Spirocyclic enedione 57 is available. The goal is to complete the total synthesis of spiroetherone A (7a).
thought.gap: Need to convert the enedione part to a dihydronaphthoquinone.
thought.decision: Diels–Alder cycloaddition of 57 with 1-acetoxybutadiene followed by treatment with sodium carbonate in ethanol to promote elimination and aromatization was performed.

outcome.observation: The compound obtained did not match the 1H and 13C NMR spectra of spiroetherone A. However, X-ray diffraction confirmed that the originally assigned structure of spiroetherone A (7a) was synthesized.

evidence.text_span: Diels–Alder cycloaddition of 57 with 1-acetoxybutadiene followed by treatment with sodium carbonate in ethanol to promote elimination and aromatization[50] led to a compound that unfortunately did not match the 1H and 13C NMR spectra of spiroetherone A. Nevertheless, the structure 7a of our synthetic compound was resolved via X-ray diffraction which confirmed that we synthetized the originally assigned structure of spiroetherone A.

thought.background: The synthesis of the originally assigned structure of spiroetherone A (7a) was successful, but it did not match the natural product's NMR data. This indicates a misassignment of the natural product's structure.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize the revised structure of spiroetherone A, which is presumed to be an epimer at the spirocyclic carbon.
thought.decision: Proposed structure 58 for spiroetherone A, where the configuration of the spirannic center is inverted. This requires accessing quinol 59 (epimer of dysideanone E (5b)) to form enedione 60 (epimer of 57) due to the stereospecificity of the rearrangement.

outcome.observation: Proposed a revised structure for spiroetherone A (58) and outlined a strategy to synthesize its epimer.

evidence.text_span: We propose structure 58 for spiroetherone A in which the configuration of the spirannic center is inverted matching the one found in septosones B, C (6a,b). In theory, accessing quinol 59, the epimer of dysideanone E (5b), should enable the formation of enedione 60, epimer of 57, due to the stereospecificity of the rearrangement.

thought.background: The goal is to synthesize quinol 59, the epimer of dysideanone E (5b).
thought.gap: Need to invert the configuration of dysideanone E (5b).
thought.decision: Attempted to invert the configuration of dysideanone E (5b) via a Mitsunobu reaction or an SN2 reaction on a triflate of dysideanone E, as well as oxidation of the phenol in dysideanone G using a chiral hypervalent iodonium reagent.

outcome.observation: Attempts to invert the configuration of dysideanone E (5b) via a Mitsunobu reaction or an SN2 reaction on a triflate of dysideanone E were unsuccessful, as well as oxidation of the phenol in dysideanone G using a chiral hypervalent iodonium reagent.

evidence.text_span: Unfortunately, attempts to invert the configuration of dysideanone E (5b) via a Mitsunobu reaction or a SN2 reaction on a triflate of dysideanone E were unsuccessful, as well as oxidation of the phenol in dysideanone G using a chiral hypervalent iodonium reagent.

thought.background: Direct inversion of the configuration of dysideanone E (5b) was unsuccessful. The goal is to invert the configuration of spirocycle 57 into 60.
thought.gap: Need an alternative sequence to invert the configuration of the spirocycle.
thought.decision: The inversion of the configuration of spirocycle 57 into 60 was achieved via a sequence involving the reduction of both carbonyls under Luche conditions, selective protection of the more accessible alcohol as a silyl ether, and acetylation of the second alcohol into 61.

outcome.observation: Compound 61 was obtained after reduction, selective protection, and acetylation.

evidence.text_span: Consequently, the inversion of the configuration of spirocycle 57 into 60 was achieved via a sequence involving the reduction of both carbonyls under Luche conditions, selective protection of the more accessible alcohol as a silyl ether, acetylation of the second alcohol into 61.

thought.background: Compound 61 is available.
thought.gap: Need to remove the silyl protecting group and oxidize the allylic alcohol to yield γ-acetoxyenone 62.
thought.decision: Removal of the silyl protecting group with TBAF and oxidation of the allylic alcohol with MnO2 were performed.

outcome.observation: γ-acetoxyenone 62 was yielded.

evidence.text_span: Removal of the silyl protecting group and oxidation of the allylic alcohol yielded γ-acetoxyenone 62.

thought.background: γ-acetoxyenone 62 is available.
thought.gap: Need to reduce 62 to the corresponding deconjugated enone and isomerize it to enone 63.
thought.decision: Reduction with zinc and acetic acid in methanol was performed, followed by isomerization with DBU.

outcome.observation: Enone 63 was delivered.

evidence.text_span: It was followed by reduction with zinc and acetic acid in methanol into the corresponding deconjugated enone which was isomerized with 1,8-diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU) to deliver enone 63.

thought.background: Enone 63 is available.
thought.gap: Need to form the vinyl triflate and perform palladium-catalyzed reduction to yield diene 64.
thought.decision: Formation of the vinyl triflate from 63 with LDA and PhNTf2 and its palladium-catalyzed reduction with formic acid were performed.

outcome.observation: Diene 64 was yielded.

evidence.text_span: Formation of the vinyl triflate from the latter with lithium diisopropylamide (LDA) and PhNTf2 and its palladium-catalyzed reduction with formic acid yielded diene 64.

thought.background: Diene 64 is available.
thought.gap: Need to perform a [4+2]-cycloaddition with singlet oxygen to form the endoperoxide.
thought.decision: Diene 64 was subjected to [4+2]-cycloaddition with singlet oxygen.

outcome.observation: Endoperoxide 65 was formed.

evidence.text_span: The latter readily underwent a [4+2]-cycloaddition with singlet oxygen.

thought.background: Endoperoxide 65 is available.
thought.gap: Need to cleave the O─O bond to form diol 66.
thought.decision: Cleavage of the O─O bond of endoperoxide 65 was performed.

outcome.observation: Diol 66 was formed.

evidence.text_span: Cleavage of the O─O bond of the endoperoxide 65 led to diol 66

thought.background: Diol 66 is available.
thought.gap: Need to oxidize diol 66 to afford the desired enedione 60.
thought.decision: Diol 66 was oxidized with Dess-Martin periodinane.

outcome.observation: The desired enedione 60 was afforded.

evidence.text_span: which was subsequently oxidized with Dess-Martin periodinane to afford the desired enedione 60.

thought.background: Enedione 60 is available. The goal is to synthesize the revised structure 58 of spiroetherone A.
thought.gap: Need to perform cycloaddition with 1-acetoxybutadiene followed by aromatization.
thought.decision: Cycloaddition with 1-acetoxybutadiene followed by aromatization was performed.

outcome.observation: The first synthesis of the revised structure 58 of spiroetherone A was enabled, whose 1H and 13C NMR data matched those of the natural product. Single X-ray diffraction further confirmed the structure.

evidence.text_span: As previously, cycloaddition with 1-acetoxybutadiene followed by aromatization enabled the first synthesis of the revised structure 58 of spiroetherone A, whose 1H and 13C NMR data matched those of the natural product. Single Xray diffraction further confirmed the structure, resolving any remaining uncertainty.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for Dactyloquinone A is not explicitly stated, but the longest linear sequence is 14 steps.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Longest linear sequence for Dactyloquinone A.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total steps for Dactyloquinone A is not explicitly stated.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: One failed attempt for Dactyloquinone A synthesis.

validation.significance: The total synthesis of dactyloquinone A was achieved via an intramolecular MHAT process, demonstrating a novel C-O bond formation. The synthesis of spiroetherone A involved a quinol–spiroenedione rearrangement and led to the revision of the natural product's structure, which was confirmed by X-ray diffraction and matching NMR data. This work provides access to complex meroterpenoids and highlights the utility of MHAT and rearrangement strategies in natural product synthesis.

source.title: Total Synthesis of Dactyloquinone A and Spiroetherone A via a Metal-Hydride Hydrogen Atom Transfer (MHAT) Process and a Quinol-Enedione Rearrangement.
source.authors: Schoenn G, Kouklovsky C, Guillot R, Magauer T, Vincent G.
